channel.title: Dodam - Issues
channel.description: A gentle fortnightly letter on balcony gardening and growing at a humane pace

item.title: The Fig Tree's Second Spring
item.description: A rooted fig cutting that did almost nothing its first year finally budded out this spring. Two years, it turns out, was always the honest timeline.
item.content:encoded:
Two Junes ago I rooted a fig cutting in a jar of water, more out of curiosity than expectation, and moved it into soil once the roots looked real. Its first year was a disappointment by every visible measure — a handful of leaves, no real height. This spring, its second, it broke dormancy with more new growth in three weeks than the whole previous year combined.

What the quiet year was actually for

The gap between those two years is the truest thing I can tell you about growing something long-lived in a container. Cuttings and young woody plants spend their early seasons building a root system proportional to the container they are in, largely out of sight, before they commit real energy to top growth. A fig that looks stalled above the soil line in year one is very often not stalled at all below it — and pulling it out to check, the temptation I mostly resisted, disturbs exactly the structure it needs to finish. I gave it a slightly larger pot at the end of that first year, more out of hope than evidence, top-dressed with fresh compost, and otherwise left it through winter with reduced watering and no fertilizer, which is the standard dormancy care for a fig that has dropped its leaves and is doing nothing you can see.

Reading dormancy correctly

A dormant fig looks, to an inexperienced eye, exactly like a dead one: bare branches, no leaves, no obvious sign of life. The distinction is in the wood — a light scratch on a young branch with a fingernail should reveal green, moist tissue underneath the bark, not brown and dry. I checked this twice over the winter, mostly for my own reassurance, and both times found green, which was the only real evidence I had that anything was still happening at all. Watering through dormancy stays minimal, just enough to keep the roots from fully drying out, never enough to keep the soil consistently moist the way an actively growing plant would need.

What this spring looked like

New growth started at the branch tips in early April, small and tentative at first, then genuinely fast through May — new leaves unfurling every few days, the kind of pace that makes a plant look different each morning. I repotted it once more in late May, sizing up modestly the way the last letter described, and by this issue it has more leaf surface than its entire first year produced, with the beginnings of what might, if this second-year pattern holds, be its first fruit next year rather than this one.

The pace this letter keeps trying to describe

A fig tree does not know that a balcony gardener wants results within one growing season. It grows on a timeline set by its own biology, and the only real choice available to the person tending it is whether to work with that timeline or fight it. This one asked for two quiet years before it did anything worth writing about, and both of those years were, it turns out, required — not wasted, not a sign anything had gone wrong, just root work that had not yet become visible. That is most of what this letter has been trying to say since the first issue, said now by a plant instead of by me: growth at the pace something actually needs is still growth, even in the seasons it does not show.

item.title: Herbs That Forgive You
item.description: Mint, perilla, and chives will survive almost anything you do wrong on a balcony — which makes them the right place to begin, or to recover.
item.content:encoded:
After four letters about getting things precisely right, I want to spend this one on the plants that make most of that precision optional. Mint, perilla (kkaennip), and chives have survived every mistake I have made on this balcony, including several weeks of outright neglect last summer.

Mint needs a container within a container

Light audits, hardening off, watering discipline, careful repotting — none of it, with these three, turns out to matter nearly as much as I once thought.

Mint's famous vigor is not a myth, and on a balcony where it cannot escape into open ground, that vigor just means it will fill and eventually crowd out anything sharing its pot. The fix is simple: keep mint in its own pot, always, even if the pot looks comically large for one small plant at first. If you want mint alongside other herbs, sink the mint's own pot into the soil of a larger shared container rather than planting it directly — the plastic or terracotta wall it stays inside contains the roots without anyone seeing the trick. I learned this after mint colonized an entire half-barrel meant for three different herbs in a single growing season.

Perilla wants shade you think it doesn't need

Most herb-growing advice defaults to "full sun," and perilla technically tolerates it, but on my hottest, most exposed afternoons it does visibly better with some relief — dappled light, or a few hours of shade from a taller neighboring plant. Left in full, unbroken afternoon sun through a heat wave, its broad leaves can scorch at the edges; moved to a spot with midday shade, the same plant fills out lush and glossy within a couple of weeks. It is one of the few plants on this balcony that has taught me to distrust the phrase "full sun" as a blanket instruction rather than a starting point to adjust from.

Chives ask almost nothing

Of the three, chives are the closest thing to unkillable in my experience. They tolerate inconsistent watering, partial shade, poor soil, and repeated harsh cutting — in fact, cutting chives back hard, close to the base, tends to reward you with fresh, tender growth rather than punishing the plant, which is the opposite of how most herbs respond to heavy pruning. A neglected pot of chives that looks finished in early spring will often come back within two weeks of a single deep watering and a hard trim.

Why this matters beyond the three plants

If a season goes badly — and by this point in the letter's life, several of my seasons have — these are the plants I recommend falling back on, not because they are lesser choices but because they let you keep gardening, keep the daily habit of stepping outside and looking closely at something growing, through the weeks when more demanding plants would only produce more evidence of failure. Confidence rebuilds faster with a thriving pot of chives than with a struggling tomato, and the habits you keep through the easy plants are the ones that carry over when you are ready to try the harder ones again.

item.title: Repotting in the Heat
item.description: Late spring root-bound plants and early-summer heat arrive at the same time. Here is how to repot without shocking either the plant or yourself.
item.content:encoded:
Late May is when the root-bound plants finally announce themselves — water running straight through without the soil absorbing it, growth stalled despite decent light, roots circling the surface. It is also, reliably, when daytime temperatures start climbing past comfortable repotting weather. Those two facts collide every year, and every year I have to relearn the same handful of adjustments to do it safely.

Confirm it is actually root-bound first

Slide the plant out of its pot — supporting the base, not pulling by the stem — before assuming anything. A healthy root system shows white or light tan roots with some visible soil still between them. A root-bound one shows a dense mat of roots circling the inside edge of the pot with little to no soil visible, sometimes pushing the whole root ball into the pot's exact shape. If what you see looks more like the first description, the plant likely needs something else entirely this week — shade, water, patience — not a new pot.

Size up modestly, not dramatically

The instinct in a heat wave is to give a struggling plant a much bigger home, reasoning that more room solves more problems. It usually does the opposite. A pot much larger than the current root system holds more soil than the roots can use, which stays wet longer than the plant wants, which invites rot right as the plant is already stressed by heat. Two to four centimeters of additional diameter is the standard, reliable step up — enough room for a season of new growth without leaving a waterlogged margin the roots cannot reach.

Choose the coolest hours available

Repot in the early morning or the evening, never in direct midday sun, and if a heatwave is forecast for the days immediately following, it is worth waiting for a cooler window even if that means living with a slightly cramped pot a little longer. A plant just disturbed at the roots has temporarily reduced capacity to take up water, exactly when hot weather is asking the most of it. Giving it a shaded, sheltered day or two after repotting — even moving it out of direct sun briefly if your setup allows — closes that gap.

Water it in properly, then leave it

A thorough watering immediately after repotting settles the new soil around the roots and closes air pockets that would otherwise dry roots out unevenly. After that, resist checking on it daily; a repotted plant in early summer heat typically needs about a week of slightly closer attention to watering, not a full return to your normal schedule, and definitely not fertilizer, which can burn a root system that has not finished settling.

None of this is complicated, but all of it is easy to skip when a plant looks distressed and the instinct is to fix it immediately. The fix, again, mostly involves doing less, more carefully.

item.title: On Overwatering as an Act of Anxiety
item.description: Overwatering is rarely about the plant needing water. It is usually about the gardener needing to do something.
item.content:encoded:
More balcony plants die from overwatering than from underwatering, by a wide margin, and I do not think this is really a knowledge problem. Most people who overwater can recite the finger test — water only if the soil comes out dry — and do it anyway, because watering is doing something, and doing something feels better than sitting with the discomfort of not knowing.

The tell in my own watering can

I noticed the pattern in myself before I could name it: I watered more on the days I felt anxious about something unrelated to the balcony entirely — a deadline, an argument, a piece of bad news — regardless of whether any plant actually needed it. The watering can became a way of managing a feeling that had nothing to do with soil moisture, and the plants absorbed the consequences of a stress that was mine, not theirs. Waterlogged roots cannot take up oxygen, they begin to rot, and a plant that is drowning can look, confusingly, a lot like a plant that is thirsty — wilting leaves, a general droop — which then invites more water, which makes it worse. It is a genuinely vicious cycle, and it starts in the gardener, not the pot.

Two things that actually broke it for me

The first was switching from a schedule in my head to a physical check every time, no exceptions: lift the pot. A recently watered pot is noticeably heavier than a dry one, once you have felt the difference a few times, and this simple habit stopped more unnecessary watering than any amount of willpower did, because it replaced a feeling with a fact.

The second was accepting that most common balcony plants — tomatoes, basil, chilies, geraniums — genuinely prefer to dry out somewhat between waterings, and that a slightly wilted look in the early evening after a hot day is not an emergency. Plants droop in heat to reduce water loss through their leaves; they often recover fully once temperatures drop overnight, with no watering at all. Learning to wait until morning before deciding whether a plant actually needed water, rather than reacting to the four o'clock droop, cut my watering frequency by close to half.

What I would ask, before the watering can comes out

Is the top inch or two of soil actually dry? Does the pot feel light? And, the harder question: am I about to water because the plant needs it, or because I need to do something with my hands right now? Only one of those is a reason to water. The other is worth sitting with instead — on the balcony, ideally, where at least the discomfort has decent company.

item.title: The Tomato That Took Its Time
item.description: One cherry tomato seedling lagged three weeks behind its siblings. Rushing it would have cost more than waiting did.
item.content:encoded:
Three cherry tomato seedlings went into pots the same day, same tray, same light, same water. Two took off within a week. The third sat there, more or less unchanged, for close to three weeks. Every instinct said to intervene. I did nothing, mostly out of stubbornness, and it turned out to be the right call for the wrong reason.

What was actually happening

More fertilizer, a different spot, maybe a fresh seedling to start over with — none of it, in the end, was needed. When the slow seedling finally did start moving, the growth came fast and dense, and the root ball I eventually saw at repotting was, if anything, better established than its faster siblings' — more fine root hair, less of the coiling that comes from a plant racing to put on top growth before its roots can support it. My best guess, and it is a guess, is that this particular plant spent its slow weeks building root structure instead of leaf structure, and that the visible pause was not the absence of growth but a different kind of it, one that does not show from above.

The hardening-off lesson underneath this

This is really a letter about hardening off, the process of gradually acclimating a seedling raised indoors or under cover to outdoor conditions — sun intensity, wind, temperature swings — before leaving it out full time. Skip or rush this step and a seedling can stall exactly the way mine did, or worse, scorch and drop leaves it cannot easily replace. The standard advice is roughly a week: two hours of outdoor exposure on day one, building by an hour or two each day, always in indirect light at first, always brought back in if a cold snap or heavy wind shows up in the forecast. I had technically done this, but on the fast end of the range, and I suspect the slow seedling simply needed the fuller week, maybe closer to ten days, to adjust without setback.

What I would tell someone watching this happen to them

If a seedling is not dead — no blackened stem, no total leaf collapse, still some green and some turgor in what remains — and its neighbors from the identical batch are thriving, the most likely explanation is a difference in pace, not a difference in health. Keep the conditions consistent, keep watering on the same schedule as the others rather than compensating with extra attention, and give it real time before concluding something has gone wrong. Three weeks felt enormous while I was in it. In the life of a tomato plant that will, with luck, produce until October, it was nothing at all.

The plant did fruit, eventually, about a week after its siblings and no less generously. I could not tell you, by August, which pot had ever been the slow one.

item.title: What a Balcony Actually Needs
item.description: Before buying anything else, a real accounting of what a small balcony garden requires to begin — and what it does not.
item.content:encoded:
For the first letter: what do you actually need to begin? Less than the gardening aisle wants you to believe. One pot with a real drainage hole, a bag of decent potting mix, a plant suited to the light you actually have, and roughly six weeks of paying attention before deciding anything is wrong.

Start with the light, not the plant

Everything else — moisture meters, fourteen kinds of fertilizer, trellises for plants you have not bought yet — can wait, and mostly should. The single biggest mistake I made in my first balcony season was choosing plants I liked the idea of, then trying to force my light to accommodate them. It goes the other way. Stand on your balcony at three points in the day — morning, midday, late afternoon — for about a week, and just watch where the sun actually falls and for how long. My balcony faces southeast and gets strong direct light from roughly ten until four; a north-facing balcony one street over gets bright, indirect light all day and almost no direct sun at all. Those are two different gardens, and no amount of enthusiasm changes which plants will thrive in each.

One pot, done properly

Resist the starter kit. A single five-liter terracotta or glazed ceramic pot, with an actual hole in the bottom — not a decorative cachepot with no drainage — will teach you more in one season than five mismatched pots bought in a single trip. Terracotta breathes and dries faster, which is forgiving for people who tend to overwater; glazed ceramic and plastic hold moisture longer, which suits hotter, windier balconies. Neither is wrong. Match the material to your own habits, not to what looks best.

Potting mix, not garden soil

Bagged potting mix, not soil dug from anywhere, and not straight compost either. Garden soil compacts hard in a container and drowns roots; a proper mix stays loose and lets air reach them. If you want to economize, a general-purpose mix with a handful of perlite worked in for extra drainage covers the overwhelming majority of balcony plants without needing a different bag for each one.

Then wait

The hardest instruction in this letter is the one nobody wants: once the plant is in, water it, place it where the light audit told you to, and then leave it alone for a stretch of days rather than checking on it twice daily. A new plant needs time to settle its roots into unfamiliar mix before it can tell you anything true about whether it is happy. Judging it on day three is like judging a conversation by its first sentence.

That is the whole kit. Everything after this is refinement, and refinement is what the rest of these letters are for.
